modules.title: Getting Started with Web Development
modules.content:
## How Websites Actually Work Imagine you are sitting in a coffee shop. You open your phone, type `www.wikipedia.org` into your browser, and within seconds, the entire encyclopedia is at your fingertips. It feels like magic. But what actually happened in that fraction of a second? To build a website, you first need to understand the underlying machinery. Every website, no matter how complex, boils down to a conversation between two computers: a **client** and a **server**. ### The Client and the Server Your phone or laptop is the **client**. When you type a web address into your browser (like Chrome, Safari, or Firefox), the client acts like a customer at a restaurant placing an order. It sends a message out into the internet asking for a specific web page. At the other end of that request is a **server**. A server is just a powerful computer stored in a data center somewhere in the world, running 24/7. Its job is to listen for requests from clients. When the server hears your request, it acts like the chef in the restaurant kitchen. It gathers the necessary files for the website you requested and sends them back to your device. This conversation is called the **request-response cycle**. You request a page, the server responds with the files. ### The Three Core Languages of the Browser When the server sends those files back to your phone or laptop, your browser receives them. But your browser doesn’t inherently know how to display a website. It relies on a specific set of instructions written in three core languages: * **HTML (HyperText Markup Language):** This is the skeleton of the website. It tells the browser *what* is on the page. For example, HTML tells the browser, "This is a heading," "This is a paragraph," or "This is an image." * **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets):** This is the clothing or paint on the skeleton. It tells the browser *how* things should look. CSS says, "Make that heading blue," "Center this image," or "Make the text larger." * **JavaScript:** This is the muscle and brain. It makes the website interactive. JavaScript makes pop-ups appear, handles form submissions, and updates live data. (Don't worry about JavaScript yet—we will focus entirely on HTML for now, and add CSS later in the book). ### The Concept of Local vs. Remote Right now, if you type a web address, you are viewing a **remote** website. The files live on a remote server far away from you. However, web developers don’t build websites directly on live servers. That would be like trying to renovate a restaurant while customers are actively eating in it. Instead, developers build websites **locally**. This means all the HTML and CSS files live right on your personal computer's hard drive. You can open and edit them without needing an internet connection, and only you can see them. Later in this book, we will take your local files and put them on a remote server so the whole world can see them. For now, your goal is to set up your local workshop. ## Setting Up Your Development Environment To write a book, an author needs a word processor like Microsoft Word or Google Docs. To write code, a programmer needs a specialized text editor. You might be wondering, "Can't I just use Notepad on Windows or TextEdit on Mac?" Technically, yes. But basic text editors lack the tools that make writing code efficient and enjoyable. Programmers use **code editors**—applications designed specifically for writing code. They highlight different parts of your code in different colors, help you spot errors, and auto-complete repetitive typing. ### Installing Visual Studio Code (VS Code) The most popular, free, and beginner-friendly code editor in the world today is **Visual Studio Code** (often just called **VS Code**). It is built by Microsoft and used by professionals and hobbyists alike. Here is how to get it: 1. Open your web browser and go to **code.visualstudio.com**. 2. The website should automatically detect your operating system (Windows, Mac, or Linux). Click the large blue download button. 3. Once the file finishes downloading, open it and run the installer. 4. Follow the installation prompts. You can safely accept the default settings for now. ### Navigating VS Code When you first open VS Code, you will see a **Welcome screen**. Close that tab for now. Let’s look at the main layout: * **Activity Bar (Far Left):** This is a vertical strip of icons. The most important one for now is the top icon, which looks like two pieces of paper. This is the **Explorer**. * **Side Bar (Left):** When you click the Explorer icon, this area opens up. It acts like a filing cabinet, showing you all the files and folders in your current project. * **Editor (Center/Right):** This is the large, main area where you will actually type your code. You can open multiple files at once, and they will appear as tabs at the top of the editor. * **Status Bar (Bottom):** This gives you quick information, like what line of code you are currently on. ### Creating Your Project Folder Before we write any code, we need a place to put it. Let's create a dedicated folder for your personal website project. 1. On your computer (using Finder on Mac, or File Explorer on Windows), create a new folder on your Desktop. 2. Name the folder `my-website`. Keep the name lowercase and use a hyphen instead of a space. This is a standard practice in web development. 3. Open VS Code. 4. Go to the top menu bar, click **File**, then click **Open Folder...** (If you are on a Mac, it might just say **Open...**). 5. Find the `my-website` folder you just created, select it, and click Open. You will see the empty `my-website` folder appear in the Side Bar on the left. You are now ready to build. ## Creating Your First HTML File Inside your `my-website` folder, we are going to create your very first HTML file. 1. In the VS Code Side Bar, hover your mouse over the `my-website` folder name. You will see a few small icons appear next to it. 2. Click the icon that looks like a piece of paper with a plus sign (**New File**). 3. Type `index.html` and press Enter. **Why `index.html`?** In web development, the file name `index.html` is special. By default, when a browser visits a folder on a server, it automatically looks for a file named `index.html` to display first. It is the "front door" of your website. You should now see `index.html` open in your main editor window. It is completely empty. Let's fix that. ### The VS Code Shortcut VS Code has a built-in shortcut called **Emmet** that writes boilerplate code for you. Click inside your empty `index.html` file. Type an exclamation mark `!` and press the **Tab** key on your keyboard. Instantly, VS Code will generate a block of code that looks something like this: ```html Document ``` Don't panic if this looks overwhelming. This is the standard **boilerplate**—the minimum required code every HTML document needs to function properly. Let's break down exactly what each piece does. ## The Anatomy of an HTML Document HTML is built using **tags**. A tag is a keyword wrapped in angle brackets, like `` or ``. Tags usually come in pairs: an opening tag and a closing tag. The closing tag always has a forward slash `/` inside the brackets before the keyword. For example, an opening tag looks like ``, and its closing tag looks like ``. Everything between the opening and closing tags is the "content" that the tag applies to. Let's look at the boilerplate piece by piece. ### The Document Type Declaration ```html ``` This line is always at the very top of the document. It is not actually an HTML tag; it is an instruction to the browser. It tells the browser, "Hey, this document is written in modern HTML (specifically, HTML5)." Without it, browsers might render your website using outdated rules. ### The Root Element ```html ... ``` The `` tag is the **root element**. It wraps around absolutely everything else in the document. Think of it as the box that holds the entire website. The `lang="en"` part is an **attribute**. Attributes provide extra information about a tag. In this case, it tells the browser and search engines that the primary language of this page is English. ### The Head Section ```html Document ``` The `` section is like the engine room of the document. It contains information *about* the page, but nothing inside the `` is actually visible on the main screen of your website. * **``**: This tells the browser which character set to use. UTF-8 includes almost every character from every language in the world, ensuring your text displays correctly. * **``**: This is a crucial tag for mobile devices. It tells the browser to scale the website to fit the width of the screen it is being viewed on. * **``**: This is the only thing in the head section the user will actually see indirectly. The text inside the `<title>` tags appears in the browser tab at the very top of your screen. Right now, it says "Document". You should change this to something descriptive, like "My Personal Website". ### The Body Section ```html <body> </body> ``` Finally, we reach the `<body>`. This is where the magic happens. Everything—every piece of text, every image, every link—that you want a user to see on your screen must go *inside* the `<body>` tags. Right now, the body is empty. Let's put something in it. ## Building Your First Web Page Let's replace the empty boilerplate with a real, albeit simple, web page. Update your `index.html` file to look exactly like this: ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> <title>Jane Doe's Personal Website Hello, World! This is my first website. ``` Save the file by pressing `Ctrl + S` on Windows, or `Cmd + S` on Mac. (VS Code puts a white dot next to the file name in the tab when you have unsaved changes. The dot disappears when you save). ### Viewing Your Work in the Browser To see the fruits of your labor, you need to open this file in a web browser. 1. Minimize VS Code and find your `my-website` folder on your Desktop. 2. Double-click the `index.html` file. 3. Your default web browser will open. You should see a completely white page with the words "Hello, World! This is my first website." in the top left corner. Look at the browser tab at the top, and you will see "Jane Doe's Personal Website". **Congratulations.** You just built and viewed your first web page locally. ### Real-World Example: A Photographer's Landing Page Let's look at a slightly more realistic scenario. Imagine you are building a simple landing page for a freelance photographer named Alex. While we won't add images or complex styling yet, we can use basic HTML to structure the text that introduces Alex's business. We will use a few new tags, but focus on how they sit inside the ``. Update your `` section to look like this: ```html Alex Rivera Photography Capturing the wild moments of the Pacific Northwest. Welcome to my portfolio. I specialize in landscape and wildlife photography. Feel free to reach out for booking information. ``` Save the file and refresh your browser. You will notice a problem: the browser ignored your line breaks. "Alex Rivera Photography" and "Capturing the wild moments..." are sitting on the exact same line. The text just runs together. This is a fundamental rule of HTML: **Browsers ignore extra whitespace and line breaks in your code.** If you want text to appear on separate lines, or if you want it to look different from regular text, you must explicitly tell the browser to do so by wrapping the text in specific tags. Let's fix Alex's page using foundational HTML tags: ```html

Alex Rivera Photography

Capturing the wild moments of the Pacific Northwest.

Welcome to my portfolio. I specialize in landscape and wildlife photography. Feel free to reach out for booking information.

``` Save and refresh your browser. Now the page looks completely different. * The `

` tag is a **heading**. It makes the text large and bold, acting as the main title of the page. * The `

` tag is a **paragraph**. It creates blocks of standard-sized text and automatically adds space above and below the text, separating it from other elements. ### Real-World Example: A Student's Resume Page Let's do one more example. Imagine a computer science student named Marcus wants to build a simple text-based resume page. He wants a main title, a sub-title, and some basic text describing his skills. Here is how his `` would look: ```html

Marcus Chen

Computer Science Student at State University

About Me

I am a junior studying software engineering. I enjoy building web applications and learning new programming languages.

``` Notice the introduction of the `

` tag. HTML provides six levels of headings, from `

` (the most important, usually the main title) down to `

` (the least important). `

` is slightly smaller than `

`, acting as a sub-section title. By using `

`, `

`, and `

` tags, you are already giving your web page a logical structure that both humans and browsers can understand. ## Key Takeaways * **The Web is a Conversation:** Websites work through a request-response cycle. Your browser (the client) requests files, and a server responds by sending them back. * **HTML is the Skeleton:** HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the code used to structure content on the web. It tells the browser *what* is on the page. * **Local vs. Remote:** Developers build websites locally (on their own computer) before deploying them to a remote server for the world to see. * **VS Code is Your Workshop:** Visual Studio Code is a free, powerful code editor that provides helpful tools like syntax highlighting and Emmet shortcuts for writing code faster. * **The Boilerplate:** Every HTML document requires a specific structure: a `` declaration, an `` root element, a `` section for behind-the-scenes metadata, and a `` section for visible content. * **Tags Dictate Structure:** HTML uses tags (keywords in angle brackets) to define elements. Browsers ignore plain line breaks in your code; you must use tags like `

` for headings and `

` for paragraphs to structure your text properly.

modules.title: Structuring Content with Text and Links
modules.content:
## The Anatomy of an HTML Element In the previous chapter, you set up your development environment using VS Code, created your first `index.html` file, and saw how a browser retrieves that file from a server to display it locally. You even used Emmet to generate a basic HTML skeleton. Now, it’s time to fill that skeleton with life. When a browser downloads your HTML file, it doesn't just see a wall of text. It looks for instructions on what that text is. Is it a main title? Is it a simple paragraph? Is it a list of your hobbies? To communicate this, we use **HTML tags**. Recall that HTML stands for **HyperText Markup Language**. The "markup" part refers to those tags. Let's look at the anatomy of a standard HTML element: ```html

This is my personal website.

``` Here is how the pieces break down: * **`

`** is the **opening tag**. It tells the browser, "A paragraph starts here." * **`This is my personal website.`** is the **content**. This is the actual text the user will read. * **`

`** is the **closing tag**. The forward slash (`/`) tells the browser, "This paragraph ends here." Together, the opening tag, content, and closing tag make up an **element**. Most HTML elements follow this exact pattern, though we will meet a few exceptions later in this chapter. ## Organizing Text with Headings and Paragraphs When you land on a webpage, you instinctively scan it to find what you're looking for. You look for big text to tell you what the page is about, and smaller text to read the details. HTML provides specific elements to create this visual and structural hierarchy. ### Headings HTML offers six levels of headings, from `

` to `

`. * **`

`** is the most important heading on your page. A personal website's homepage might have an `

` with your name. * **`

`** is used for major sections beneath the main heading, like "About Me" or "Projects". * **`

`** through **`

`** are used for sub-sections, decreasing in importance and visual size as the number goes up. Think of headings like the outline of a document. You wouldn't jump from an `

` directly to an `

` in the same section, just as you wouldn't put a sub-sub-sub-section directly under the main title of an essay. ### Paragraphs For standard blocks of text, you will use the paragraph element, `

`. Browsers automatically add a bit of space above and below paragraphs to separate them visually. **Real-World Example 1: A Personal Bio Page** Let's say you are building the "About Me" section of your personal website. Here is how you might structure the headings and paragraphs: ```html

Jamie Rivera

Welcome to my personal website. I am a recent computer science graduate based in Seattle, Washington, with a passion for frontend development.

My Background

I have been coding for three years. I love solving puzzles and creating interfaces that are easy for everyday people to use.

Education

I graduated from the University of Washington with a B.S. in Computer Science in 2023.

``` Notice the logical step-down: the main name is an `

`, the major section is an `

`, and the specific sub-topic is an `

`. *Note: Browsers ignore extra spaces and line breaks in your code. If you hit "Enter" twice between two paragraphs in your code editor, the browser will still just display them with standard paragraph spacing. The visual layout is determined by the tags, not the whitespace in your code.* ## Grouping Content with Lists Lists are a fantastic way to present information clearly, especially on a personal portfolio. HTML gives you two main types of lists to work with. ### Unordered Lists An **unordered list** is used when the order of the items doesn't matter. Visually, browsers render these with bullet points. You create an unordered list using the `
    ` tag. However, the `
      ` tag is just a container. Inside it, you place **list items** using the `
    • ` tag. ```html

      My Hobbies

      • Photography
      • Rock climbing
      • Playing the guitar
      ``` ### Ordered Lists An **ordered list** is used when the sequence of the items is important, like a step-by-step tutorial or a ranking. Browsers automatically number these items (1, 2, 3...). You create this using the `
        ` tag, again filling it with `
      1. ` elements. ```html

        My Morning Routine

        1. Brew a pot of coffee
        2. Check my email and calendar
        3. Write a list of three goals for the day
        ``` ## The Power of the Web: Hyperlinks The "HT" in HTML stands for **HyperText**. HyperText is simply text that contains links to other text. Without hyperlinks, the web would just be a collection of isolated, unconnected documents. Links are what allow users to jump from your "About Me" page to your GitHub profile, or from an article you wrote to a source you cited. To create a link, we use the **anchor element**, written as ``. If you just write `Click here`, the browser will display the text, but it won't know *where* to send the user when they click it. To provide that information, we need to use **attributes**. ### Understanding HTML Attributes Attributes are extra pieces of information added to an HTML opening tag to configure the element or change its behavior. An attribute always consists of two parts: 1. **The name:** What kind of setting are you adjusting? 2. **The value:** What is the specific setting? (Always wrapped in straight quotation marks `"`). For an anchor element, the most critical attribute is **`href`** (short for "hypertext reference"). The `href` attribute tells the browser the destination URL of the link. ```html View my GitHub ``` In this example: * `` closes the opening tag. * `View my GitHub` is the visible, clickable text (often called the anchor text). * `` closes the element. ### Controlling Where Links Open Sometimes, you want a link to open in the current browser tab, replacing your website. Other times, you want the link to open in a brand new tab so the user doesn't lose their place on your site. You control this behavior using another attribute: **`target`**. The default behavior of a link (if you don't include a `target` attribute) is to open in the same tab. If you want the link to open in a new tab, you set the target attribute to `_blank`: ```html Connect with me on LinkedIn ``` Now, when a user clicks "Connect with me on LinkedIn,"the LinkedIn page will load in a new browser tab, keeping your personal website open in the background. An element can have multiple attributes; just separate them with a space inside the opening tag. **Real-World Example 2: A Social Media Directory** Let’s build a small section for your website that links out to all your professional profiles. We'll combine headings, paragraphs, lists, and links. ```html

        Find Me Online

        I'm active on several platforms. Feel free to reach out!

        • GitHub
        • LinkedIn
        • Twitter
        ``` Notice how the `` tags are placed *inside* the `
      2. ` tags. This is called **nesting**—putting one element inside another. It’s perfectly valid and extremely common to nest HTML elements to create the structure you need. ### Absolute vs. Relative Links So far, all of our `href` values have started with `https://`. These are called **absolute links**. They contain the full, complete web address, including the protocol (`https://`) and the domain name (`github.com`). You use absolute links when you are pointing to a completely different website. However, you will also want to link to other pages *within your own website*. For example, you might want a link on your homepage that goes to your `contact.html` page. When linking to pages on your own site, you use **relative links**. A relative link does not include the `https://` or the domain name. It simply provides the path to the file relative to the page you are currently on. ```html Contact Me ``` If a user clicks this link, the browser will look for a file named `contact.html` in the exact same folder as the current page. We will explore relative links in much more depth when we build multi-page navigation in a later chapter, but for now, it’s important to know the difference between linking to the outside world (absolute) and linking to your own files (relative). ## Special Elements: Line Breaks and Horizontal Rules As we mentioned earlier, browsers ignore extra whitespace and line breaks in your code. If you want to force text onto a new line without starting a completely new paragraph (which adds extra vertical spacing), you can use the **line break** element: `
        `. Notice something missing from `
        `? There is no closing tag. Some HTML elements do not have closing tags because they do not wrap around content. These are sometimes called "void" or "empty" elements. You just type `
        ` wherever you want the line to break. Similarly, if you want to draw a horizontal line across the page to separate sections, you use the **horizontal rule** element: `
        `. Like the line break, it is an empty element with no closing tag. **Real-World Example 3: Formatting a Resume Page** Let’s look at how you might use `
        ` and `
        ` to format a simple resume or CV page on your site. ```html

        Work Experience

        Frontend Developer Intern

        Tech Solutions Inc.
        June 2022 - August 2023
        Seattle, WA

        During my internship, I was responsible for:

        • Updating the company's customer portal using HTML and CSS.
        • Fixing 20+ UI bugs reported by users.

        ``` In this example, the `
        ` puts a clean line under the main "Work Experience" heading. The `
        ` tags inside the `

        ` element keep the company name, dates, and location grouped tightly together in a single paragraph block, rather than spacing them out into three separate paragraphs. Notice that we also nested an unordered list (`

          `) inside a paragraph (`

          `). While this works, it's generally best practice to close your paragraph tag before starting a list, like this: ```html

          During my internship, I was responsible for:

          • Updating the company's customer portal using HTML and CSS.
          • Fixing 20+ UI bugs reported by users.
          ``` HTML is very forgiving and will often still display the first version correctly, but writing clean, properly nested code ensures browsers and screen readers interpret your content exactly as you intend. ## Putting It All Together You now have the tools to take the blank `index.html` file you created in Chapter 1 and turn it into a structured, readable homepage. By combining headings, paragraphs, lists, and hyperlinks, you can organize your thoughts and guide your visitors through your content. Remember, HTML is about structure and meaning. Don't worry just yet about making the text a specific color, changing the font, or adjusting the size of your headings—that is the job of CSS, which we will cover in Chapter 4. For now, focus on choosing the correct HTML element for the correct piece of content. Is it a main title? Use `

          `. Is it a block of standard text? Use `

          `. Is it a list of links? Use `

            ` and ``. ## Key Takeaways * **HTML elements** consist of an opening tag (`

            `), content, and a closing tag (`

            `). * Use **headings** (`

            ` through `

            `) to create a logical outline and hierarchy for your page, not to make text bigger. * Use the **paragraph** (`

            `) element for standard blocks of text. * **Unordered lists** (`

              `) create bulleted lists, while **ordered lists** (`
                `) create numbered lists. Both contain **list item** (`
              1. `) elements. * The **anchor** (``) element creates hyperlinks. * **Attributes** provide extra information to HTML elements. They consist of a name and a value (e.g., `name="value"`). * The **`href`** attribute tells an anchor element where to link. * The **`target`** attribute controls how a link opens. Use `target="_blank"` to open a link in a new browser tab. * **Absolute links** (starting with `https://`) point to other websites. **Relative links** (just the file name, like `contact.html`) point to pages within your own website. * Empty elements like `
                ` (line break) and `
                ` (horizontal rule) do not have closing tags.

modules.title: Adding Visuals with Images and Media
modules.content:
## Why Visuals Matter Imagine walking into an art gallery where every wall is completely blank, and the curator simply hands you a typed list of descriptions of the paintings. You’d likely feel disappointed and leave. A webpage without images or media feels exactly the same way. In the previous chapters, you built a solid foundation using text and links. You learned how to structure content with HTML (HyperText Markup Language) so your browser understands what is a heading, what is a paragraph, and what is a link. But text alone rarely tells the whole story. Whether you want to showcase a profile photo of yourself, display a gallery of your graphic design work, or embed a video reel of your coding projects, visual media is what transforms a plain document into an engaging experience. In this chapter, we will take your personal website to the next level by adding images and external media. You will learn how to place images on your page, how to make those images accessible to everyone, and how to embed videos from platforms like YouTube directly into your site. ## Inserting Images with the `` Tag In HTML, images are not actually "pasted" into a web page like you might paste a picture into a Word document. Instead, your HTML file simply contains a set of instructions that tells the browser where to find the image file and how to display it. To add an image, we use the **`` tag**. The word "img" is just an abbreviation for "image." Unlike the `

` (paragraph) or `

` (heading) tags you used in Chapter 2, the `` tag is an **empty element** (sometimes called a void element). This means it doesn't wrap around any text content, so it doesn't need a closing tag. You won't write ``; instead, the entire tag is self-contained. Because the tag itself doesn't contain any text, the browser needs two crucial pieces of information—provided as **attributes**—to make the image appear. Attributes are extra bits of information added inside the opening tag to configure the element. The two most important attributes for the `` tag are `src` and `alt`. ### The `src` Attribute: Pointing to the File The **`src` attribute** (short for "source") tells the browser exactly where to find the image file. Just like the `href` attribute you used for links in Chapter 2, the `src` attribute relies on a URL (Uniform Resource Locator) to locate the resource. There are two main ways to provide this source: locally or remotely. **1. Local Images** In Chapter 1, you learned about working locally on your computer. If you have an image file saved on your computer—like a photo of yourself named `profile.jpg`—you can tell the browser to look for it inside your website folder. If your `index.html` file and your `profile.jpg` image are in the exact same folder, the `src` value is simply the file name: ```html ``` **2. Remote Images** You can also point to an image that lives on a remote server somewhere else on the internet. As long as you have the full web address of the image, you can use it. ```html ``` When the browser loads your page, it will send a request to that remote server to fetch the image and display it on your site. ### The `alt` Attribute: Accessibility and Fallbacks The **`alt` attribute** (short for "alternative text") provides a text description of the image. It might seem like an optional extra, but it is absolutely essential for building a modern, professional website. Here is why the `alt` attribute is so important: * **Accessibility:** Many internet users rely on **screen readers**—software programs that read the text on a screen out loud for people who are blind or visually impaired. When a screen reader encounters an `` tag, it cannot "see" the picture. Instead, it reads the `alt` text aloud. Without it, visually impaired users have no idea what is on the screen. * **Broken Links:** Sometimes, images fail to load. This could happen if a remote server goes down or if you accidentally misspell the file name. If an image doesn't load, the browser will display the `alt` text in its place, giving the user context about what they are missing. * **Search Engine Optimization (SEO):** Search engines like Google read text, but they cannot easily "see" images. Good `alt` text helps search engines understand what your images are about, which can help your site rank higher in search results. #### Writing Good `alt` Text Writing effective alternative text is a skill. The goal is to be descriptive but concise. * **Do:** Describe the content and purpose of the image. * **Don't:** Start with "Image of..." or "Picture of...". The screen reader already announces that it is an image. * **Do:** Keep it under 100 characters if possible. * **Don't:** Stuff it with keywords for SEO. Write for humans. If an image is purely decorative and adds no real meaning to the content (like a background swoosh or a decorative divider), you should still include the `alt` attribute, but leave it empty: `alt=""`. This tells the screen reader to skip it entirely. ### Putting It All Together Here is a complete, real-world example of an `` tag you might use for a portfolio website: ```html ``` Notice that in this example, the `src` points to a folder named `images` inside the website directory, then to the file `my-artwork.jpg`. Keeping your images organized in a dedicated folder is a best practice we will explore next. ## Organizing Your Project Files As you build your personal website, you will quickly accumulate multiple files. If you mix your HTML files, your image files, and your CSS files all in one single folder, your project will become messy and confusing. Best practice dictates that you create sub-folders to keep things organized. A typical website folder structure looks like this: * **website-folder/** * `index.html` * **images/** * `profile.jpg` * `artwork.png` * **css/** * `style.css` When your image is inside a folder, you must include the folder name in your `src` path, followed by a forward slash (`/`). To display the `profile.jpg` file from the example above, your HTML would look like this: ```html ``` If you ever move the image or rename the folder, you must update the `src` attribute to match, or the image will break. ### Understanding Image File Types Not all image files are created equal. Different formats are optimized for different types of visuals. When downloading or exporting images for your website, you will typically encounter three main formats: * **JPEG (or .jpg):** Best for photographs and images with millions of colors and complex shading. JPEGs compress the file size, which helps your web page load faster, but they do not support transparency. * **PNG:** Best for graphics, logos, and icons. PNGs support transparency (meaning you can see the background behind the image), but the file sizes are usually larger than JPEGs. * **GIF:** Best for simple animations. GIFs only support a limited color palette, so they aren't great for detailed photos. * **SVG:** Stands for Scalable Vector Graphics. Unlike the other formats, SVGs are made of mathematical code rather than pixels. They are perfect for logos and icons because they never lose quality, no matter how much you zoom in. For your personal website, you will likely use JPEGs for your profile photo and background pictures, and PNGs or SVGs for any logos or icons. ## Embedding External Media with ` ``` Unlike the `` tag, the `` tag. However, if you just use the `src` attribute, the iframe will default to a very small size, and it might look awkward on your page. To control how the iframe looks, we use a few more attributes: * **`width` and `height`:** These attributes define the dimensions of the iframe window in pixels. * **`title`:** Just like the `alt` attribute on an image, the `title` attribute provides a text description of the iframe's content for screen readers. This is crucial for accessibility. Here is a more complete, real-world example of embedding a YouTube video: ```html ``` ### The Easier Way to Embed Media Memorizing iframe attributes isn't necessary. Almost every platform that allows embedding (YouTube, Vimeo, Google Maps, CodePen) provides the exact code you need. Here is how you do it on YouTube: 1. Navigate to the video you want to embed. 2. Click the **Share** button below the video. 3. Click the **Embed** option. 4. YouTube will generate a block of ` ``` *Note: The `allowfullscreen` attribute in the example above is a boolean attribute. It doesn't need an equals sign or a value. Its mere presence tells the browser to allow the user to click the fullscreen button on the video player.* ## Common Pitfalls and How to Fix Them When working with media for the first time, things occasionally go wrong. Here are the most common issues beginners face and how to solve them. **1. The Broken Image Icon** If you see a little torn picture icon instead of your image, your browser cannot find the file. * **The Fix:** Double-check your `src` path. Did you spell the file name exactly right? File names are case-sensitive, so `Profile.jpg` is different from `profile.jpg`. If your image is in a folder, ensure you included the folder name (e.g., `images/profile.jpg`). **2. Huge Images Breaking Your Layout** You took a photo on your phone and put it on your website, but it is so massive it takes up the entire screen and you have to scroll for miles just to get past it. * **The Fix:** While we will learn how to resize images perfectly using CSS in Chapter 4, you can prevent massive files from slowing down your site by resizing the actual image file before uploading it. Use a free tool or your computer's built-in photo editor to scale the image down to a reasonable width (like 1000 or 1500 pixels wide) before adding it to your `images` folder. **3. Missing `alt` or `title` Attributes** Your images load, but you forgot the accessibility text. * **The Fix:** Always go back and add `alt` to images and `title` to iframes. Make it a habit from day one. A website without accessible media is a website that excludes users. ## Key Takeaways * Use the **`` tag** to add images to your webpage. It is an empty element, meaning it does not have a closing tag. * The **`src` attribute** tells the browser where to find the image file, either locally in your project folder or remotely on a server. * The **`alt` attribute** provides alternative text for screen readers and broken links. It is a mandatory practice for web accessibility. * Keep your project organized by storing images in a dedicated sub-folder, and reflect that folder path in your `src` attribute (e.g., `src="images/photo.jpg"`). * Use **JPEGs** for photographs and **PNGs** for graphics or images requiring transparency. * The **`

modules.title: Introduction to CSS and Styling Text
modules.content:
## The Visual Layer of the Web In the previous chapters, you built the structural foundation of your personal website using HTML. You added text, links, and images. Right now, your site probably looks like a standard, unstyled document from the 1990s—black text on a white background, using the browser's default font, with everything aligned to the left side of the screen. HTML tells the browser *what* content is (a heading, a paragraph, an image). **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)** tells the browser *how that content should look*. If HTML is the skeleton of your website, CSS is the skin, clothing, and personal style. CSS allows you to change text colors, swap out fonts, adjust sizes, and eventually control the entire layout of your page. By the end of this chapter, your personal website will undergo a visual transformation, taking on a distinct personality through custom typography and color. ## Linking CSS to HTML Before you can style your HTML, you have to tell your HTML where to find the styling instructions. While there are a few ways to apply CSS, the industry standard is to use an **external stylesheet**. This means you write your CSS in a completely separate file, keeping your structure (HTML) and your design (CSS) cleanly separated. ### Creating Your CSS File Open your project folder in VS Code. In the Explorer panel, create a new file and name it `style.css`. The `.css` file extension tells VS Code and the browser that this file contains Cascading Style Sheets. Your folder structure should now look something like this: * `index.html` * `style.css` * (and any image files you added previously) ### The `` Element To connect this new file to your HTML, you need to use the `` element inside the `` of your `index.html` document. Add the following lines to the `` of your HTML file, making sure to save both files afterward: ```html Your Name - Personal Website ``` Let’s break down that `` tag: * **`rel="stylesheet"`**: The `rel` attribute defines the relationship between the current document and the linked file. Here, we are declaring it is a stylesheet. * **`href="style.css"`**: Just like the links you built in Chapter 2, the `href` attribute points to the file's location. Because `style.css` is in the exact same folder as `index.html`, we only need to provide the file name. If you open your `index.html` in your browser right now, nothing will have changed. That’s because your `style.css` file is completely empty. Let’s fix that. ## CSS Syntax and Selectors To write CSS, you need to learn its basic grammar. A CSS rule is made up of two main parts: the **selector** and the **declaration block**. Here is what a basic CSS rule looks like: ```css p { color: blue; } ``` * **`p`** is the **selector**. It tells the browser which HTML elements to style. In this case, we are targeting all paragraph (`

`) elements. * **`{ }`** contains the **declaration block**. Everything inside these curly braces will apply to the selector. * **`color: blue;`** is a **declaration**. A declaration consists of a **property** (`color`) and a **value** (`blue`), separated by a colon, and ending with a semicolon. Think of it like a form you are filling out: Property: "What do you want to change?" Value: "What should it change to?" ### Type Selectors The most straightforward way to style your website is by using a **type selector**. A type selector targets HTML elements by their tag name. If you want all your top-level headings to be a specific color, you use the `h1` selector: ```css h1 { color: darkgreen; } h2 { color: teal; } ``` Type selectors are powerful, but they have a limitation: they apply to *every* instance of that element on the page. If you have three `

` elements, they will all turn teal. What if you only want one of them to be teal, and another to be orange? That’s where classes come in. ### Class Selectors When you need to style specific elements without affecting all the others of the same type, you use a **class selector**. First, you add a `class` attribute to your HTML element. Let's say your personal website has an "About Me" section and a "Projects" section, both using `

` tags. ```html

About Me

Welcome to my personal website...

Projects

Here are some things I have built...

Contact Me

Send me an email!

``` To target only the elements with the class `section-title` in your CSS, you use a period (`.`) followed by the exact class name: ```css .section-title { color: orange; } ``` Now, "About Me" and "Projects" will be orange, but "Contact Me" will remain the default black. You can apply the same class to as many elements as you want, across different tag types. You could put `class="section-title"` on a paragraph, a list, or a heading, and the CSS rule above would apply to all of them. ## Applying Basic Text Styles Now that you know how to target elements, let's look at the specific properties you can use to format your text. We will focus on three foundational properties: `color`, `font-family`, and `font-size`. ### Changing Text Color The `color` property changes the color of the text itself. CSS gives you several ways to define colors, but the two most common for beginners are **named colors** and **hexadecimal colors**. **Named colors** are exactly what they sound like—words like `red`, `blue`, `teal`, `tomato`, or `goldenrod`. They are great for quick testing, but they limit you to a small, predefined list of options. **Hexadecimal colors** (often called "hex codes") are six-character codes preceded by a hash symbol (`#`). They allow you to pick from millions of precise colors. The code is made up of three pairs of numbers or letters representing Red, Green, and Blue (RGB) values. * `#000000` is pure black. * `#FFFFFF` is pure white. * `#FF0000` is pure red. * `#3A5FCD` is a specific shade of blue. You can use either method in your CSS: ```css h1 { color: #2C3E50; /* A dark, slate blue */ } p { color: #333333; /* A very dark gray, softer than pure black */ } ``` *Note: In CSS, you can leave comments for yourself or other developers by wrapping text in `/*` and `*/`. The browser ignores anything inside these markers.* ### Setting the Font Family The `font-family` property dictates which typeface the browser should use to display your text. Because you are building a website that will be viewed on different computers and devices, you cannot guarantee that every user has the same fonts installed. To handle this, CSS allows you to provide a "font stack"—a list of fonts in order of preference. The browser will try the first font; if the user doesn't have it installed, it moves to the next, and so on. ```css body { font-family: "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif; } ``` In this example: 1. The browser looks for "Helvetica Neue". 2. If it's not found, it looks for "Arial". 3. If neither is found, it defaults to whatever `sans-serif` font the operating system uses. It is a best practice to always end your font stack with a generic family name like `serif` (fonts with little feet, like Times New Roman) or `sans-serif` (fonts without feet, like Arial) as a fallback. Notice that we applied this to the `body` selector. CSS uses **inheritance**. Most text-related properties applied to the `` tag are automatically inherited by all the text elements inside it (headings, paragraphs, lists). By setting the font on the body, you set the default font for the entire page in one line of code. ### Adjusting Font Size The `font-size` property controls how large or small your text appears. You can use several units of measurement in CSS, but the most common for text are pixels (`px`) and `rem`. **Pixels (`px`)** are an absolute unit. If you set `font-size: 24px;`, the text will be 24 pixels tall. While straightforward, pixels don't always scale gracefully if a user changes their browser's default font size for accessibility. **`rem` (root em)** is a relative unit. One `rem` is equal to the default font size of the root element (usually the `` tag), which browsers typically set to 16 pixels. Therefore, `1rem` equals `16px`, `2rem` equals `32px`, and `0.5rem` equals `8px`. Using `rem` is highly recommended because it respects the user's browser settings, making your site more accessible. ```css html { font-size: 16px; /* Setting the root size explicitly */ } h1 { font-size: 2.5rem; /* 40px */ } h2 { font-size: 1.75rem; /* 28px */ } p { font-size: 1rem; /* 16px */ } ``` ## Real-World Example: Styling a Personal Bio Page Let’s put these concepts together into a practical scenario. Imagine you are building the landing page for your personal website. You have your main heading, a short introductory paragraph, and a distinct call-to-action paragraph inviting people to read your blog. Here is your HTML structure: ```html Jane Doe - Web Developer

Jane Doe

Welcome to my corner of the internet. I am a junior developer learning to build for the web.

Check out my latest projects!

``` Right now, this is plain. Let's open `style.css` and apply what we've learned to give it a polished, modern feel. ```css /* style.css */ /* Set the base font and text color for the whole page */ body { font-family: "Trebuchet MS", Helvetica, sans-serif; color: #2d2d2d; } /* Style the main name heading */ h1 { color: #8A2BE2; /* A vibrant blue-violet */ font-size: 3rem; } /* Make the intro paragraph slightly larger than default */ .intro { font-size: 1.25rem; color: #555555; /* A medium gray */ } /* Make the call-to-action stand out */ .call-to-action { color: #FF4500; /* Orange-red */ font-size: 1.5rem; } ``` If you save both files and refresh your browser, the transformation is immediate. The page no longer looks like a default browser document. Jane's name is large and purple. The intro text is readable and slightly gray, making it less aggressive than pure black. The call-to-action is a larger, bright orange, naturally drawing the visitor's eye. ## Real-World Example: Creating a Consistent Article Style Consistency is the hallmark of good design. On a personal website, you might have a section featuring short articles or blog posts. You want every article to look identical in its typography. Let's assume your HTML has multiple articles, each with a title and some text: ```html

My Journey Learning HTML

It all started in Chapter 1 of my web development book...

Adding Images to My Site

In Chapter 3, I learned how to embed media...

``` Instead of writing CSS for every single heading and paragraph individually, you use classes to create a reusable style system: ```css .article-title { font-family: Georgia, "Times New Roman", serif; color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 1.8rem; } .article-body { font-family: Georgia, "Times New Roman", serif; color: #333333; font-size: 1.1rem; } ``` By applying the `.article-title` and `.article-body` classes, you guarantee that every article you ever add to your site will have the exact same serif typography (Georgia), sizing, and coloring. If you ever decide to change your site's article font to a modern sans-serif font, you only have to change it in one place in your CSS file, and every article updates instantly. This separation of structure and style is what makes CSS so powerful. ## Key Takeaways * **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)** controls the visual presentation of your HTML structure. * An **external stylesheet** (a separate `.css` file) is the best way to write CSS. You link it to your HTML using the `` tag inside the ``. * A CSS rule consists of a **selector** (what to style) and a **declaration block** (how to style it). * **Type selectors** (like `p` or `h1`) target all elements of a specific type. * **Class selectors** (like `.intro`) target only elements that have a matching `class` attribute in the HTML, allowing for highly specific, reusable styling. * The `color` property changes text color and accepts named colors (like `blue`) or hexadecimal codes (like `#3A5FCD`). * The `font-family` property sets the typeface. Always provide a "font stack" (e.g., `"Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif`) ending in a generic family as a fallback. * The `font-size` property controls text size. Using relative units like `rem` ensures your text scales well with user browser settings. * Applying text styles to the `body` element leverages **inheritance**, passing those styles down to all child text elements automatically.

modules.title: Mastering the CSS Box Model
modules.content:
## The Invisible Boxes Around Everything When you look at a well-designed webpage, text seems to breathe. Images sit perfectly spaced away from the edges of the screen, and distinct sections of content are easy to tell apart. But when you build your first webpage using just HTML, it often looks like a cramped, endless block of text touching the very edges of the browser window. The reason for this drastic difference comes down to a fundamental concept in CSS. Every single element on a webpage—whether it’s a paragraph, a heading, an image, or a link—is treated by the browser as a rectangular box. Even if an element looks round or irregular on the screen, the browser still calculates its size and position using a strict, invisible rectangle. Understanding how this rectangle is constructed is the secret to controlling the layout, spacing, and overall readability of your personal website. In web development, we call this concept the **CSS Box Model**. ## Anatomy of the Box Model The Box Model describes the space an element takes up by breaking that invisible rectangle down into four distinct layers. Working from the inside out, these layers are: **content**, **padding**, **border**, and **margin**. Let’s define what each layer does. ### 1. Content The content is the innermost part of the box. This is where your actual text, images, or other media live. When you set a width or height on an element using CSS, you are usually setting the size of this content area. If you write a paragraph of text, the words themselves make up the content. ### 2. Padding Padding is the space *inside* the box, sitting between the content and the border. Think of padding as the bubble wrap or the inner cushioning of a package. If you add a background color to an element, that color will fill both the content area and the padding area. Padding pushes the content away from the edges of the box. ### 3. Border The border is the visible outline of the box. It wraps directly around the content and the padding. You can make a border thick, thin, solid, dotted, or completely invisible. Even if a border is set to zero pixels wide, it still exists mathematically as the dividing line between the inside of the box (content and padding) and the outside of the box (margin). ### 4. Margin Margin is the transparent space *outside* the border. It is the invisible force field that pushes this box away from other elements on the page. Because margins are transparent, whatever background color is on the webpage will show through the margin. Margins are primarily used to create gaps between separate elements. ## Calculating Box Size Here is where most beginners run into their first major layout frustration. By default in CSS, the size of an element is calculated by adding up the content, padding, and border. If you tell a box to be 200 pixels wide, the browser makes the *content* exactly 200 pixels wide. If you then add 20 pixels of padding and a 5-pixel border, the actual visible width of that box on the screen becomes 250 pixels (200 content + 20 left padding + 20 right padding + 5 left border + 5 right border). This means every time you add padding or a border, your element gets larger, which can easily break a carefully planned layout. Fortunately, CSS provides a property to change this default behavior. ### The Magic of `box-sizing: border-box` To make sizing elements predictable, web developers almost universally apply a CSS rule called `box-sizing`. When you set an element to `border-box`, you are telling the browser: "Make the total width of this element exactly what I say, and shrink the content area to make room for any padding or borders." If you set a box to 200 pixels wide with `box-sizing: border-box`, and then add 20 pixels of padding and a 5-pixel border, the total box remains 200 pixels wide. The browser simply reduces the content area to 150 pixels to accommodate the padding and borders. In your CSS stylesheet, it is highly recommended to apply this to every single element on your page using the universal selector (`*`): ```css * { box-sizing: border-box; } ``` With this single line of code at the top of your CSS file, you never have to do complex math to figure out how wide an element will actually be on the screen. ## Applying Spacing to Improve Readability Now that you know what the layers are, how do you actually use them? Spacing is the difference between a website that feels professional and one that feels claustrophobic. Let's look at how to use padding and margins to improve the readability of your personal website. ### Working with Padding Padding is applied using the `padding` property. You can apply the same padding to all four sides of a box at once, or target specific sides individually. ```css /* Adds 20 pixels of space on all four sides */ .callout-box { padding: 20px; } /* Adds 10px top and bottom, 30px left and right */ .profile-card { padding: 10px 30px; } ``` Padding is incredibly useful when you have a box with a background color and you want the text inside it to not touch the edges. Without padding, text sits right on the line of the border, which is very difficult to read. ### Working with Margins Margins are applied using the `margin` property, and the syntax works just like padding. However, margins serve a different purpose: creating separation between entirely different elements. ```css /* Creates a gap of 40 pixels below this heading */ .section-title { margin-bottom: 40px; } ``` **Real-World Example: The Blog Post** Imagine you are writing an "About Me" page. You have a main heading (`

`), a sub-heading (`

`), and three paragraphs of text (`

`). By default, browsers apply standard margins to these text elements, but they are often too tight for a modern, breathable design. You might want a large gap between your main heading and your sub-heading to signal a new section, but a smaller gap between your paragraphs so they read as a single connected thought. ```css h1 { margin-bottom: 30px; /* Large gap below the main title */ } h2 { margin-bottom: 15px; /* Medium gap below the sub-heading */ } p { margin-bottom: 10px; /* Smaller gap between paragraphs */ } ``` ### The Margin Collapse Quirk There is one specific behavior with margins that often catches beginners off guard. It is called **margin collapse**. Vertical margins (top and bottom) behave differently than horizontal margins (left and right). If you have two elements stacked on top of each other, and the bottom element has a bottom margin of 30 pixels, while the top element has a top margin of 20 pixels, the gap between them will *not* be 50 pixels. Instead, the larger of the two margins wins. The browser "collapses" the two margins into a single 30-pixel gap. This only happens with vertical margins on block-level elements. Horizontal margins never collapse, and padding never collapses. If you need to guarantee a specific gap between two stacked elements without worrying about collapse, you can use padding on one of the elements instead. ## Using Background Colors to Distinguish Sections When building a personal website, you will often want to break your page into distinct visual sections. For example, your homepage might have a dark section introducing who you are, followed by a white section listing your projects, and finally a dark section with your contact details. Background colors are applied using the `background-color` property. It is crucial to remember that background colors only fill the **content** and **padding** areas of the box model. They stop exactly at the **border**. The **margin** remains completely transparent. ### Real-World Example: Highlighting a Call to Action Let’s say you want to add a "Hire Me" button to your website. You want it to have a solid background color, but if the text touches the edges of that color, it looks cramped. ```css .hire-me-button { background-color: #2563eb; /* A nice blue color */ color: white; /* Makes the text readable on the blue background */ padding: 12px 24px; /* Gives the text room to breathe inside the button */ border: 2px solid #1e40af; /* A slightly darker blue border */ margin-top: 20px; /* Pushes the button away from the text above it */ } ``` In this example: 1. The `background-color` fills the space behind the text and extends out to the edge of the padding. 2. The `padding` ensures the white text isn't crashing into the edges of the blue background. 3. The `border` provides a crisp, darker outline around the entire blue area. 4. The `margin` creates empty space between the button and whatever element sits above it on the page. ### Distinguishing Full-Width Sections If you want to create a full-width band of color across the entire browser window to separate your "Projects" section from your "Contact" section, you apply a background color and padding to the container holding that section's content. ```css .projects-section { background-color: #f3f4f6; /* A light gray background */ padding: 60px 20px; /* 60px top and bottom pushes the content away from the edges of the gray band */ margin-bottom: 0; /* Prevents margin collapse from adding unwanted white space below the gray band */ } ``` By adding 60 pixels of top and bottom padding, you ensure the text inside the projects section has plenty of breathing room, making it highly readable. The background color stretches edge-to-edge across the screen, clearly distinguishing it from the white sections above and below it. ## Key Takeaways - Every element on a webpage is treated as a rectangular box, consisting of four layers: **content**, **padding**, **border**, and **margin**. - **Content** is the text or media itself. **Padding** is the inner space between the content and the border. **Border** is the outline of the box. **Margin** is the transparent outer space that separates the box from other elements. - By default, adding padding and borders increases the total visible size of an element. Applying `box-sizing: border-box` to all elements forces the browser to include padding and borders within the specified width, making layouts much easier to manage. - Background colors only fill the content and padding areas; they do not extend into the margins. - Vertical margins can "collapse" when stacked on top of each other, meaning the larger margin overrides the smaller one rather than the two adding together. - Strategic use of padding (for inner breathing room) and margins (for separating distinct elements) is the primary way to improve text readability and visual hierarchy on your personal website.

modules.title: Structuring a Personal Website Layout
modules.content:
## The Blueprint of a Web Page Imagine trying to assemble a complex piece of furniture without the instruction manual. You have all the wooden panels, screws, and brackets, but no idea how they fit together. You might end up with a wobbly table that collapses the moment you put a coffee cup on it. Building a website without a clear structure leads to a similar disaster. In previous chapters, you learned how to write text, add images, and style them using the CSS Box Model. You know how to build the individual pieces of the furniture. Now, we need to learn how to read the blueprint and put those pieces together so the final product is sturdy, logical, and visually coherent. When building a personal portfolio website, your goal is to present information about yourself in a way that is easy for visitors (and potential employers) to digest. To do this, web developers don’t just throw content onto a page; they organize it into distinct, predictable sections. Before we write a single line of code, we need to plan the layout. ## Creating a Wireframe Every well-built website starts with a **wireframe**. A wireframe is a basic, low-fidelity visual guide that represents the skeletal framework of a web page. Think of it as a rough sketch or a blueprint. It doesn't include colors, fancy fonts, or actual images. Instead, it uses simple boxes and placeholder text to map out where major pieces of content will live. Wireframes are crucial because they force you to think about the hierarchy and organization of your information before you get distracted by the visual design. ### Sketching Your Personal Portfolio You don't need expensive software to create a wireframe. A pen and a piece of paper are often the best tools for the job. Let’s sketch out a standard layout for a personal portfolio website. Grab a piece of paper and draw a large rectangle representing your browser window. Now, divide that rectangle into the following logical sections from top to bottom: 1. **The Top Bar:** Draw a thin rectangle across the very top. This will hold your name or logo on the left, and a few text links (like "About", "Projects", and "Contact") on the right. 2. **The Hero Section:** Draw a large box directly beneath the top bar. This takes up most of the screen when a visitor first loads the page. It usually contains a large greeting ("Hi, I'm Alex!"), a short sentence about what you do, and maybe a prominent button. 3. **The About Me Section:** Draw a wide rectangle below the hero. This might have a smaller box inside it on the left for a profile picture, and a larger box on the right for a paragraph of text. 4. **The Projects Section:** Draw another large rectangle. Inside it, draw three smaller, equally sized boxes side-by-side. These will hold your portfolio pieces. 5. **The Bottom Bar:** Draw a final thin rectangle at the very bottom of the page. This will hold copyright information and maybe a few social media links. By sketching this out, you have just created a wireframe. You have organized your content into logical sections based on their importance and function. ## The Problem with "Divititis" In the early days of the web, developers only had one HTML tool to create these structural boxes: the generic division tag, `
`. If you wanted to create a header, you used a `
`. If you wanted a sidebar, you used a `
`. To tell them apart, developers added attributes like `class="header"` or `id="footer"`. This led to a common affliction known affectionately as "divititis"—web pages so deeply nested in `
` tags that the underlying code looked like alphabet soup. Here is what a typical layout looked like using only `
` tags: ```html
Navigation links here
Welcome to my site
Copyright 2024
``` To a human looking at the code, it takes a moment to figure out what each `
` is doing. To a web browser, it’s even harder. Browsers don't look at class names to understand the layout; they just see empty boxes. ## Enter Semantic HTML5 To solve this problem, HTML5 introduced **semantic tags**. The word "semantic" relates to meaning in language or logic. Therefore, **semantic HTML** refers to tags that clearly describe their own purpose to both the developer and the browser. Instead of using a generic `
` and naming it "header", you simply use the `
` tag. The browser immediately knows, "Ah, this is the introductory section of the page." Using semantic tags provides several massive benefits: * **Accessibility:** Screen readers (software used by visually impaired users to navigate the web) rely on semantic tags to understand the structure of a page. A blind user can easily jump directly to the main content if it is wrapped in a `
` tag. * **Search Engine Optimization (SEO):** Search engines like Google use semantic tags to understand what your page is about. If your important content is in an `
` tag, Google weighs it differently than a `
` tag. * **Code Readability:** It is much easier for you (or other developers) to read and edit a document where the tags explain themselves. Let’s look at the core semantic tags you will use to build your personal website layout. ## Building the Skeleton: Core Semantic Tags Your wireframe can be translated directly into HTML using a specific set of semantic elements. Let's break down the distinct sections of your portfolio and the tags that define them. ### The `
` Element The `
` element represents introductory content or a set of navigational links. It typically contains the website's logo or primary heading, and often wraps the main navigation. A web page can have multiple headers. You might have one at the very top of your page for the site navigation, and another `
` inside a specific blog post containing the article's title and publish date. ### The `
` Element Short for navigation, the `
` element defines a set of navigation links. Not all groups of links on a page need to be wrapped in a `
`; it is reserved for major navigation blocks, like your primary site menu. ### The `
` Element The `
` element is arguably the most important structural tag on your page. It wraps the dominant content of the ``. There should be only one `
` element per page, and it should not contain any content that is repeated across multiple pages (like site-wide headers, footers, or sidebars). Everything unique to this specific page—your hero section, your about text, your project gallery—goes inside the `
` tag. ### The `
` Element The `
` element represents a standalone, thematic grouping of content. Every section should typically have its own heading (like an `

`). If your main content area has three distinct parts—an introduction, an about me area, and a projects area—each of those should be wrapped in its own `
`. It is a way to divide your `
` content into logical chapters. ### The `
` Element The `
` element represents a self-contained composition that could, in theory, be distributed or reused independently of the rest of the page. Think of a blog post, a news story, or a product card. In your personal portfolio, each individual project in your "Projects" section could be wrapped in an `
` tag, because each project is a self-contained piece of content. ### The `
` Element The `
` element is used for content that is tangentially related to the content around it. Think of it as a sidebar. It could hold "related links", advertisements, or a short bio that sits next to your main blog posts. ### The `
` Element The `
` element represents the closing section of a page, section, or article. It typically contains information about the author, copyright data, links to terms of service, or contact details. Like the header, you can have multiple footers on a page (e.g., a footer for the whole site, and a footer at the end of a specific blog post). ## Putting It All Together: A Real-World Example Let’s translate the wireframe we sketched earlier into actual HTML. We will build the complete skeleton for a personal portfolio website. Notice how we still use `
` tags for generic grouping (like putting three project cards into a row), but we rely on semantic tags for the major structural landmarks. ```html Jane Doe's Portfolio

Jane Doe

  • About
  • Projects
  • Contact

Hi, I'm Jane. I build beautiful web experiences.

I am a front-end developer based in Seattle.

View My Work

About Me

I love turning complex problems into simple, beautiful interfaces. When I'm not coding, you can find me hiking in the Cascades or experimenting with new coffee roasts.

My Projects

Weather App

A JavaScript application that fetches real-time weather data.

Recipe Box

A digital cookbook built with HTML and CSS.

Pomodoro Timer

A productivity timer to help you stay focused.

© 2024 Jane Doe. All rights reserved.

  • GitHub
  • LinkedIn
``` ### Breaking Down the Code Let's look at why this structure is so powerful: 1. **The `
`** clearly establishes the top of the document. Inside it, we place our name in an `

` tag, and wrap our primary links inside a `
` tag. A screen reader will announce "Navigation" when it reaches this block. 2. **The `
` tag** wraps everything else. If a visually impaired user visits your site, their screen reader will offer them a shortcut to jump directly to the `
` content, skipping the header navigation entirely. 3. **The `
` tags** divide our main content into logical chunks. We use the `id` attribute (`id="about"`, `id="projects"`) to create anchor points. This allows the links in our `
` bar to jump the user down to the correct section when clicked. 4. **The `
` tags** inside the projects section represent self-contained pieces of content. If you wanted to feature one of your projects on another website, you could copy that `
` block, and it would make perfect sense standing on its own. 5. **The `
`** sits at the bottom, containing copyright information and a secondary `
` for social media links. ## A Second Example: A Blog Post Layout To see how semantic tags adapt to different types of content, let’s look at how you might structure a personal blog post. Notice how we still use `
` and `
`, but in a slightly different context. ```html

My Journey Learning to Code

Published on October 15, 2024

When I first started learning HTML, I was overwhelmed by the number of tags...

But once I discovered semantic HTML, everything clicked into place.

Tip:

Always use semantic tags over divs when possible!

Today, I build full websites from scratch...

Written by Jane Doe. Follow me on Twitter for more coding tips.

``` In this example, the `
` and `
` are placed *inside* the `
` tag. This tells the browser, "This is the header for this specific blog post," not the header for the entire website. This demonstrates the flexibility of semantic HTML: the tags describe the structure of whatever content they are wrapping. ## Adding Basic Structure Styling While we will cover advanced layout techniques (like Flexbox and Responsive Design) in later chapters, your semantic HTML actually provides some basic structure on its own. However, by default, block-level elements like `
`, `
`, and `
` will take up the full width of the screen, but they will stack directly on top of each other with no space between them. Using the CSS Box Model knowledge you gained in the previous chapter, you can add a little bit of basic styling to make your semantic structure visible. Create a CSS file and link it to your HTML. Add the following basic styles to give your layout some breathing room: ```css /* Give the whole page a clean background and readable font */ body { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin: 0; padding: 0; background-color: #f4f4f9; color: #333; } /* Style the site header */ header { background-color: #333; color: white; padding: 20px; text-align: center; } /* Remove default bullet points from navigation lists */ nav ul { list-style-type: none; padding: 0; } /* Make navigation list items sit side-by-side */ nav ul li { display: inline-block; margin-right: 15px; } /* Style the main content area to center text and add spacing */ main { max-width: 800px; margin: 20px auto; /* Centers the main block on the page */ padding: 20px; background-color: white; border-radius: 8px; } /* Add space below each section */ section { margin-bottom: 40px; } /* Style the footer */ footer { background-color: #333; color: white; text-align: center; padding: 10px; margin-top: 40px; } ``` Even without advanced CSS layouts, this basic styling leverages the Box Model to create a visually distinct layout. The `header` and `footer` now have dark backgrounds that stretch across the screen, clearly defining the top and bottom of your page. The `main` content is given a `max-width` and centered using `margin: 20px auto`, creating a readable column of text. The `section` tags are given bottom margins to separate the "About" and "Projects" areas visually. ## Common Pitfalls to Avoid As you begin structuring your own website, keep these common beginner mistakes in mind: * **Nesting `
` inside a `
`:** The `
` element should be a direct child of the `` tag. Don't hide it inside other wrapper elements. * **Using `
` as a generic spacer:** Do not use `
` tags just to create empty space or apply background colors. A section should always contain meaningful content, usually headed by an `

` or `

`. * **Wrapping everything in a `
`:** Only use the `
` tag for major navigation menus. If you just have a couple of random links inside a paragraph, do not wrap them in a `
`. * **Forgetting the `
`:** Even if your footer is just one line of text saying "Made by Me," wrap it in a `
` tag. It completes the semantic structure of the page. ## Key Takeaways * **Wireframing first:** Always sketch your layout on paper before writing code. It helps you organize content into logical sections before dealing with syntax. * **Semantic HTML provides meaning:** Semantic tags like `
`, `
`, and `
` describe their purpose to the browser, unlike generic `
` tags which only act as empty boxes. * **Accessibility and SEO:** Using semantic tags improves your site's search engine ranking and makes it navigable for users relying on screen readers. * **The core structural tags:** * `
` for introductory content or top navigation. * `
` for major link menus. * `
` for the dominant, unique content of the page (use only one!). * `
` for thematic groupings of content within the main area. * `
` for self-contained, reusable pieces of content. * `
` for closing information like copyrights and contact links. * **Structure enables styling:** By organizing your HTML semantically, you create clear targets for your CSS, allowing you to use the Box Model to add spacing, backgrounds, and visual hierarchy to your portfolio.

modules.title: Creating a Multi-Page Navigation System
modules.content:
## From Single Page to Multi-Page Websites Right now, your personal website lives entirely in a single HTML file. If you followed along with *Structuring a Personal Website Layout*, you likely have a landing page with a header, a main content area, and a footer, all styled beautifully using the CSS Box Model. But a real personal website needs to do more than just look good on one page. You need an "About" page to share your story, a "Projects" page to showcase your work, and a way for visitors to seamlessly jump between them. Think about the last time you visited a coffee shop. The barista doesn’t hand you a single massive sheet of paper containing the history of the company, the full menu, and the employee handbook all mashed together. Instead, they hand you a concise menu. If you want to know the company's history, you look at the "About Us" section on their website. If you want to see job openings, you navigate to a completely different "Careers" page. Websites work exactly the same way. Trying to cram your portfolio, your life story, and your contact information into a single `index.html` file creates a massive, scrolling nightmare for your users. Instead, we need to break our content into separate, digestible HTML files and build a **navigation system** to connect them. ## The Anatomy of a Multi-Page Website In a multi-page website, every distinct piece of content gets its own HTML file. When a user clicks a link to visit a new page, their browser initiates a new **request-response cycle**. The browser sends a **request** to the **server** asking for the new HTML file, the **server** finds that file and sends it back as a **response**, and the browser renders the new page on the user's **client** device. To make this work smoothly, we need to establish three things: 1. **Separate HTML files** for each section of your site. 2. **A consistent navigation bar** that appears on every page. 3. **Relative file paths** so the browser knows exactly where to find your other pages on your computer (or **locally**). Let’s start by creating the files. ### Creating Your Website's File Structure Open **Visual Studio Code** (VS Code) and open the folder where your current website lives. In the **Explorer** pane of the **Side Bar**, you should already have an `index.html` file. Why is it called `index.html`? By long-standing convention, web servers look for a file named `index.html` by default when someone visits the root of a website. It is your home base. Let's create two new files right alongside it: 1. In the VS Code Explorer, click the "New File" icon. 2. Name it `about.html` and press Enter. 3. Click "New File" again, name it `projects.html`, and press Enter. Your file structure in the Explorer should now look like this: * `index.html` * `about.html` * `projects.html` * `style.css` (or whatever you named your CSS file earlier) Right now, `about.html` and `projects.html` are completely blank. Let's fix that by adding some basic boilerplate HTML to `about.html`: ```html About Me

About Me

Hi, I'm learning to code and this is my personal website.

``` Notice that we are linking to `style.css` in the ``. Because this new file is in the exact same folder as your CSS file, the link works exactly the same way it did in your `index.html` file. Go ahead and add similar boilerplate to `projects.html` (changing the title and heading to "Projects"). ## Understanding Relative File Paths In *Structuring Content with Text and Links*, you learned how to create hyperlinks using the anchor (``) tag. You probably linked to other websites, which use **absolute paths**—URLs that include the full web address, like `https://www.example.com`. To link your own pages together, you don't use full web addresses. Instead, you use **relative file paths**. A relative file path tells the browser where a file is located *relative* to the page the user is currently viewing. Because `index.html`, `about.html`, and `projects.html` are all sitting next to each other in the exact same folder, linking between them is incredibly simple. You just use the file name. ### Example 1: Linking Pages in the Same Folder To link from your home page (`index.html`) to your projects page (`projects.html`), you would write: ```html View My Projects ``` When a user clicks this link, the browser looks in the same folder as `index.html`, finds `projects.html`, and loads it. ### Navigating Subfolders As your website grows, you won't want every single file dumped into one giant folder. You might want to organize your images into an `images` folder, or your project pages into a `projects` folder.
