Imagine your file structure looks like this: * `index.html` * `style.css` * `images/` (a folder) * `profile.jpg` * `projects/` (a folder) * `project-one.html` If you are writing code in `index.html` and you want to link to `project-one.html`, you have to tell the browser to look *inside* the `projects` folder first. You do this by including the folder name, followed by a forward slash (`/`), followed by the file name: ```html View Project One ``` If you are writing code in `project-one.html` and want to link back to `index.html`, you have to tell the browser to go *up* one level out of the `projects` folder. You do this using two dots and a slash (`../`), which translates to "go up one folder": ```html Back to Home ``` For now, our personal website is simple enough that all three HTML files live in the same folder, so we can just use the file names directly. ## Building a Consistent Navigation Bar A navigation bar is the map for your website. Without it, users are stranded on whatever page they happen to land on. A good navigation bar should be: * **Consistent:** It should look identical and appear in the exact same place on every page. * **Clear:** It should use obvious text labels like "Home", "About", and "Projects". * **Accessible:** It should use standard HTML tags so screen readers and browsers can understand it. ### The `
` Element HTML provides a specific semantic tag for navigation: `
`. While you could technically just use a `
`, the `
` tag tells the browser and assistive technologies, "This specific section of the page is for navigating the website." Let's build our navigation bar. We will use an unordered list (`
    `) containing list items (`
  • `), and we will place our anchor (``) tags inside the list items. Using a list is a web standard because it logically groups the navigation links together. ### Example 2: The HTML Structure for a Navigation Bar Open your `index.html` file. Right below the opening `` tag (or inside your header area if you built one in the last chapter), add the following code: ```html
    • Home
    • About
    • Projects
    ``` Now comes the crucial part of a multi-page website: **consistency**. You must copy this exact block of HTML and paste it into the exact same location in your `about.html` and `projects.html` files. If you add a "Blog" page next week, you will have to open all four HTML files and manually add the Blog link to the `
    ` in each one. This is one of the quirks of building purely with HTML and CSS—as sites get huge, frameworks and templating languages are used to inject the navigation automatically. But for our personal site of three to five pages, copying and pasting the HTML is perfectly fine and standard practice. ## Styling the Navigation Bar with CSS Right now, your navigation bar looks like a standard bulleted list stacked vertically. It works, but it doesn't look like a website menu. We need to use CSS to transform it. Because we want this navigation bar to look identical on every page, we will write the CSS once in our `style.css` file. Since all three HTML files link to `style.css`, the styles will automatically apply everywhere. First, let's remove the default bullet points from the unordered list and get rid of the default spacing. Then, we will use a CSS property called `display: inline-block` to make the list items sit side-by-side horizontally instead of stacking vertically. ### Example 3: CSS for a Horizontal Navigation Bar Open your `style.css` file and add the following rules: ```css /* Remove bullets and default padding from the list */ nav ul { list-style-type: none; margin: 0; padding: 0; } /* Make the list items sit side-by-side */ nav li { display: inline-block; margin-right: 20px; } /* Style the links themselves */ nav a { text-decoration: none; color: #333333; /* Dark gray text */ font-weight: bold; } /* Change the color when the user hovers over a link */ nav a:hover { color: #0066cc; /* Blue text on hover */ } ``` Let’s break down what this CSS is doing: * `list-style-type: none;` strips away the circular bullets from your `
      `. * `display: inline-block;` changes the `
    • ` elements from taking up the full width of the page (block-level) to only taking up the width of their content, allowing them to flow next to each other horizontally. * `margin-right: 20px;` adds a little breathing room between each menu item so the words aren't crashing into each other. * `text-decoration: none;` removes the default underline from your hyperlinks. * `nav a:hover` is a **pseudo-class**. It tells the browser, "When the user's mouse is hovering over this link, apply these specific styles." Changing the text color on hover is a great way to show the user that the text is clickable. Save your `style.css` file and refresh your `index.html` page in the browser. You should now see a sleek, horizontal navigation bar. Because you pasted the `
      ` HTML into your other pages, and they all share the same CSS file, the navigation bar will look identical on `about.html` and `projects.html` too. ## Indicating the "Active" Page There is one subtle but important detail missing from our navigation system. When a user is on the "About" page, the navigation bar should give them a visual cue that they are currently on the About page. In web design, this is called the **active state**. Because we are using static HTML, we can't automatically tell the browser which page is active. Instead, we have to manually add a class to the correct link on each page. Let's add a class called `active` to our links. **In `index.html:** ```html
      • Home
      • About
      • Projects
      ``` **In `about.html:** ```html
      • Home
      • About
      • Projects
      ``` **In `projects.html:** ```html
      • Home
      • About
      • Projects
      ``` Now, we need to style that `active` class in our `style.css` file so it stands out from the rest of the links. We can give it a different color, or even a background color to make it look like a button. ```css nav a.active { color: #0066cc; /* Matches the hover color */ border-bottom: 2px solid #0066cc; /* Adds a line under the active page */ } ``` Now, if you are on the Home page, the "Home" link will have a blue underline. Click over to the About page, and the underline will move to the "About" link. This small detail dramatically improves the user experience of your website. ## Putting It All Together You now have the foundational skills to build a website of any size. By combining separate HTML files, relative file paths, and a shared CSS stylesheet, you can create a five-page website just as easily as a three-page one. Here is a quick checklist to run through when adding a new page to your website in the future: 1. Create a new `.html` file in VS Code (e.g., `contact.html`). 2. Add your HTML boilerplate, including the `` to your `style.css`. 3. Copy and paste your `
      ` block from one of your other pages. 4. Move the `class="active"` attribute to the new page's link in the navigation bar. 5. Add your unique content inside the `` of the new file. By keeping your file structure organized and your navigation consistent, your personal website will feel like a cohesive, professional product rather than a scattered collection of documents. ## Key Takeaways * **Multi-page websites** split content into separate HTML files (like `about.html` and `projects.html`) rather than cramming everything onto one page. * **`index.html`** is the standard default file name for a website's home page. * **Relative file paths** tell the browser where to find a file relative to the current page. Files in the same folder just use the file name (e.g., `href="about.html"`). To go up a folder, use `../`. * The **`
      ` element** is the semantic HTML tag used to wrap a website's primary navigation links. * **Consistent navigation** requires copying and pasting the `
      ` HTML block into the same location on every page of your site. * Using an **unordered list (`
        `)** inside the `
        ` is the standard way to structure navigation links. CSS (`display: inline-block`) is used to arrange them horizontally. * An **active state** can be created by manually adding a class (like `class="active"`) to the link of the page the user is currently on, and styling that class differently in your CSS.

modules.title: Responsive Design with Flexbox
Adding Polish with CSS Interactions
Deploying Your Website to the Internet
modules.content: ## From Your Computer to the World Imagine you just finished cooking a spectacular meal. You’ve spent hours perfecting the recipe, adjusting the spices, and plating it beautifully. But right now, that meal is sitting on your kitchen counter. If you want other people to taste it, you have to invite them over or send it out. Over the past nine chapters, you have been cooking. You learned how to structure content with HTML, style it beautifully with CSS, and lay it out using Flexbox. You added responsive design and polished it with smooth CSS interactions. But until now, your website has been sitting on your kitchen counter—your local computer. When you open an HTML file from your computer, it lives **locally**. The files are physically stored on your hard drive. If you text a friend a screenshot of your website, they can see what it looks like, but they cannot click the links or interact with it. To let anyone in the world experience your website, you need to put it on a **server**—a powerful computer specifically designed to run 24 hours a day, waiting to send your website files to anyone who asks for them. This process of moving your local files to a remote server is called **deployment**. By the end of this chapter, you will have a live, public **URL** (Uniform Resource Locator, commonly known as a web address) that you can share with friends, family, and potential employers. ## Understanding Local vs. Hosted Files To understand deployment, we need to look at the **request-response cycle** from a new angle. When you double-click an HTML file on your computer, your web browser (the **client**) doesn't need to ask a server for anything. It simply reads the file directly from your hard drive. That’s why the address bar usually looks something like `file:///Users/yourname/Documents/website/index.html`. This is local hosting. When you deploy a website, you are moving your files to a **host**—a company that owns and maintains servers. Once your files are hosted, the request-response cycle changes. Here is what happens when a friend types your live URL into their browser: 1. **The Request:** Your friend’s browser sends a message out to the internet asking, "Please send me the files for this website." 2. **The Server Responds:** The host's server receives the request, finds your HTML, CSS, and image files, and sends them back. 3. **The Render:** Your friend's browser receives the files and pieces them together, displaying your beautifully styled personal website. Because the server is always turned on and connected to the internet, anyone can make this request at any time, from anywhere in the world. ## Choosing Your Hosting Platform There are many companies that will host your website, but for your first personal website, you want a platform that is free, reliable, and built specifically for static websites. A **static website** is a site made entirely of HTML, CSS, and perhaps a little JavaScript. It doesn’t have a database or complex server-side processing. Because static websites are lightweight, they are incredibly cheap (often free) to host. Two of the most popular free options for beginners are **GitHub Pages** and **Netlify**. Both are excellent, but they approach the deployment process slightly differently. ### GitHub Pages GitHub is a website used by developers to store and track changes to their code. Because it's a place to store code, they offer a feature called GitHub Pages, which turns your stored code into a live website. **Pros of GitHub Pages:** * It introduces you to Git and GitHub, which are industry-standard tools used by almost all professional developers. * It is completely free. * It is highly stable. **Cons of GitHub Pages:** * The setup process involves a few more technical steps, including downloading separate software to push your code to the internet. ### Netlify Netlify is a platform built specifically for deploying modern websites. It is designed to make the transition from local files to a live URL as frictionless as possible. **Pros of Netlify:** * You can deploy your site simply by dragging and dropping your project folder from your computer into your web browser. * It provides instant feedback—if your HTML has a broken link, Netlify will often warn you. * It offers incredibly fast deployment times. **Cons of Netlify:** * It hides the underlying version control systems, meaning you don't get the same introduction to professional developer workflows that you get with GitHub. For the rest of this chapter, we will walk through the deployment process using Netlify, as its drag-and-drop interface is the most intuitive for beginners taking their first steps into publishing. However, the concepts you learn here apply universally to all hosting platforms. ## Preparing Your Project for the Public Before you upload your files, you need to make sure your project is neatly organized. If you recall from earlier chapters, your files should be organized in a main project folder. Let's call ours `my-portfolio`. Right now, your `my-portfolio` folder probably contains: * An `index.html` file (your homepage) * Other HTML files for your multi-page navigation system (like `about.html` or `contact.html`) * A CSS folder containing your stylesheet (like `style.css`) * An images folder containing your visuals and media ### The Importance of `index.html` When you open a local file, you can click on any HTML file to view it. But when a server receives a request for a website (like `www.yoursite.com`), it doesn't know exactly which file to serve first. By industry standard, servers are programmed to look for a file named exactly `index.html`. When someone types your URL, the server automatically searches for and delivers `index.html`. If you named your homepage `home.html` or `main.html`, the server won't know what to do, and your visitors will see an error page. **Checklist before deploying:** * Ensure your main homepage is named `index.html` (all lowercase). * Check that your file paths are correct. If you used relative paths like `` and ``, they will work perfectly online. If you accidentally used paths that point to specific places on your hard drive (like `C:/Users/name/Desktop/css/style.css`), the website will break online. * Remove any large, unused image files to keep your website loading quickly. ## Deploying with Netlify: A Step-by-Step Guide Let’s walk through the exact process of getting your site live using Netlify’s drag-and-drop feature. ### Step 1: Create an Account Open your web browser and go to **netlify.com**. Click the "Sign Up" button. You can create an account using an email address, or you can use a GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket account if you already have one. ### Step 2: Navigate to the Deploy Interface Once you are logged in, look at the left-hand sidebar. Click on the **Sites** tab. You will see a button that says **Add new site**. Click it, and select **Deploy manually** from the dropdown menu. ### Step 3: Drag and Drop Your Files You will see a large dotted box on your screen that says "Drag and drop your site output folder here." This is where the magic happens. 1. Open your computer’s file explorer (Finder on Mac, File Explorer on Windows). 2. Navigate to where you saved your `my-portfolio` folder. 3. **Important:** Do not drag the `my-portfolio` folder itself. You need to drag the *contents* of the folder, OR drag the folder if Netlify specifically instructs you to drag the site folder. Netlify expects the `index.html` file to be at the very root (the top level) of what you upload. 4. Drag your folder into the dotted box on the Netlify website. ### Step 4: Wait for Deployment As soon as you drop the files, Netlify’s servers go to work. They receive your files and place them on a public server. This usually takes less than 30 seconds. You will see a progress bar, and then a green banner announcing that your site is live! ### Step 5: Claim Your URL Netlify will automatically generate a random, slightly silly URL for your website. It might look something like `sparkly-unicorn-1234.netlify.app`. Click on the link. A new tab will open, and there it is: your personal website, live on the internet. Send that link to a friend and have them open it on their phone to see your responsive design in action. ## Real-World Example: The Broken Image Dilemma Let’s look at a common scenario. Imagine a learner named Sarah. She built a beautiful portfolio website. She used Visual Studio Code, structured her layout perfectly, and uploaded her folder to Netlify. The deployment was successful! But when she clicked her live URL, all her text was there, but her images were broken, showing only little broken image icons. What went wrong? Sarah went back to her code in VS Code and looked at her `index.html` file. She found this line of code: `` Sarah had used an **absolute path** that pointed directly to her local hard drive. When Sarah’s friend in another state tried to view the website, their browser asked the internet for a file located on Sarah’s personal computer. Because Sarah’s computer wasn't a public server, the request failed. **The Fix:** Sarah changed the path to a **relative path**: `` She saved the file, dragged her updated folder back into Netlify, and the images instantly appeared on the live site. This is why checking your file paths before deploying is crucial. ## Real-World Example: Updating a Live Site A website is rarely "finished." A week after deploying, Sarah finishes a new project and wants to add it to her portfolio. How does she update a site that is already live? She does not need to create a new site. 1. She opens her `my-portfolio` folder locally on her computer. 2. She edits her `index.html` file in VS Code to add the new project. 3. She goes back to Netlify, clicks on her site in the dashboard, and navigates to the **Deploys** tab. 4. She drags and drops her updated folder into the same deploy box. Netlify recognizes the new files, instantly replaces the old ones, and updates the live URL. If she makes a mistake and breaks her site, Netlify even keeps a history of her previous uploads, allowing her to "roll back" to an older version with one click. ## Customizing Your Web Address While `sparkly-unicorn-1234.netlify.app` is fun, it’s not the most professional link to put on a resume. ### Changing the Subdomain The easiest way to customize your URL is to change the subdomain. In Netlify, go to **Site configuration** > **Domain settings**. You will see an option to edit your subdomain. As long as the name isn't taken by another user, you can change it to something like `sarahsmith-portfolio.netlify.app`. This is still completely free. ### Using a Custom Domain If you want a truly professional web address like `www.sarahsmith.com`, you will need to purchase a **custom domain**. A domain name is the human-readable address of a website. You buy domains through companies called **registrars** (like Namecheap, Google Domains, or GoDaddy). Buying a domain usually costs between $10 and $20 per year. Once you own a domain, you can link it to your hosted website by updating your **DNS (Domain Name System) records**. DNS is essentially the phonebook of the internet. When someone types `www.sarahsmith.com`, the DNS records tell the browser, "Go look at the Netlify server to find this website." While buying a custom domain is a great next step for a professional portfolio, it is entirely optional. Your free Netlify subdomain is perfectly fine for sharing your work while you continue to learn and build. ## Key Takeaways * **Local vs. Hosted:** Local files live on your personal computer and can only be seen by you. Hosted files live on a remote server and can be accessed by anyone on the internet via the request-response cycle. * **Static Websites:** Your HTML and CSS personal website is a static website, which means it can be hosted for free on platforms like Netlify or GitHub Pages. * **The Golden Rule of Index:** Your homepage must be named `index.html`. Servers are programmed to look for this exact filename when someone visits your root URL. * **Check Your Paths:** Always use relative paths (like `images/photo.jpg`) instead of absolute local paths (like `file:///Users/...`). Absolute paths will break when your site is uploaded to the internet. * **Updating is Easy:** Deploying updates to your site is as simple as dragging and dropping your updated project folder into your hosting platform. You can deploy as many times as you want. * **Custom Domains:** Free hosting gives you a subdomain (like `yoursite.netlify.app`). For a professional web address, you can optionally purchase a custom domain from a registrar and link it to your hosted files.

Let’s build something amazing together. --- # Module 1: Getting Started with Web Development ## How Websites Actually Work Imagine you are sitting in a coffee shop. The visual layout is determined by the tags, not the whitespace in your code.* ## Grouping Content with Lists Lists are a fantastic way to present information clearly, especially on a personal portfolio. HTML gives you two main types of lists to work with. ### Unordered Lists An **unordered list** is used when the order of the items doesn't matter. Visually, browsers render these with bullet points.         ``` ## The Power of the Web: Hyperlinks The "HT" in HTML stands for **HyperText**. HyperText is simply text that contains links to other text. Without hyperlinks, the web would just be a collection of isolated, unconnected documents. Links are what allow users to jump from your "About Me" page to your GitHub profile, or from an article you wrote to a source you cited. These are called **absolute links**. They contain the full, complete web address, including the protocol (`https://`) and the domain name (`github.com`). You use absolute links when you are pointing to a completely different website. However, you will also want to link to other pages *within your own website*. For example, you might want a link on your homepage that goes to your `contact.html` page. When linking to pages on your own site, you use **relative links**. A relative link does not include the `https://` or the domain name. It simply provides the path to the file relative to the page you are currently on. ```html Contact Me ``` If a user clicks this link, the browser will look for a file named `contact.html` in the exact same folder as the current page. We will explore relative links in much more depth when we build multi-page navigation in a later chapter, but for now, it’s important to know the difference between linking to the outside world (absolute) and linking to your own files (relative). ## Special Elements: Line Breaks and Horizontal Rules As we mentioned earlier, browsers ignore extra whitespace and line breaks in your code. You’d likely feel disappointed and leave. A webpage without images or media feels exactly the same way. In the previous chapters, you built a solid foundation using text and links. You learned how to structure content with HTML (HyperText Markup Language) so your browser understands what is a heading, what is a paragraph, and what is a link. But text alone rarely tells the whole story. Whether you want to showcase a profile photo of yourself, display a gallery of your graphic design work, or embed a video reel of your coding projects, visual media is what transforms a plain document into an engaging experience. In this chapter, we will take your personal website to the next level by adding images and external media. You will learn how to place images on your page, how to make those images accessible to everyone, and how to embed videos from platforms like YouTube directly into your site. ## Inserting Images with the `` Tag In HTML, images are not actually "pasted" into a web page like you might paste a picture into a Word document. Instead, your HTML file simply contains a set of instructions that tells the browser where to find the image file and how to display it. To add an image, we use the **`` tag**. The word "img" is just an abbreviation for "image." Unlike the `

                ` (paragraph) or `

                ` (heading) tags you used in Chapter 2, the `` tag is an **empty element** (sometimes called a void element). This means it doesn't wrap around any text content, so it doesn't need a closing tag. You won't write ``; instead, the entire tag is self-contained. Because the tag itself doesn't contain any text, the browser needs two crucial pieces of information—provided as **attributes**—to make the image appear. Attributes are extra bits of information added inside the opening tag to configure the element. The two most important attributes for the `` tag are `src` and `alt`. ### The `src` Attribute: Pointing to the File The **`src` attribute** (short for "source") tells the browser exactly where to find the image file. Just like the `href` attribute you used for links in Chapter 2, the `src` attribute relies on a URL (Uniform Resource Locator) to locate the resource. There are two main ways to provide this source: locally or remotely. **1. Local Images** In Chapter 1, you learned about working locally on your computer. If you have an image file saved on your computer—like a photo of yourself named `profile.jpg`—you can tell the browser to look for it inside your website folder. If your `index.html` file and your `profile.jpg` image are in the exact same folder, the `src` value is simply the file name: ```html ``` **2. Remote Images** You can also point to an image that lives on a remote server somewhere else on the internet. As long as you have the full web address of the image, you can use it. ```html ``` When the browser loads your page, it will send a request to that remote server to fetch the image and display it on your site. ### The `alt` Attribute: Accessibility and Fallbacks The **`alt` attribute** (short for "alternative text") provides a text description of the image. It might seem like an optional extra, but it is absolutely essential for building a modern, professional website. Here is why the `alt` attribute is so important: * **Accessibility:** Many internet users rely on **screen readers**—software programs that read the text on a screen out loud for people who are blind or visually impaired. When a screen reader encounters an `` tag, it cannot "see" the picture. Instead, it reads the `alt` text aloud. Without it, visually impaired users have no idea what is on the screen. * **Broken Links:** Sometimes, images fail to load. This could happen if a remote server goes down or if you accidentally misspell the file name. If an image doesn't load, the browser will display the `alt` text in its place, giving the user context about what they are missing. * **Search Engine Optimization (SEO):** Search engines like Google read text, but they cannot easily "see" images. Good `alt` text helps search engines understand what your images are about, which can help your site rank higher in search results. #### Writing Good `alt` Text Writing effective alternative text is a skill. The goal is to be descriptive but concise. * **Do:** Describe the content and purpose of the image. * **Don't:** Start with "Image of..." or "Picture of...". The screen reader already announces that it is an image. * **Do:** Keep it under 100 characters if possible. * **Don't:** Stuff it with keywords for SEO. Write for humans. If an image is purely decorative and adds no real meaning to the content (like a background swoosh or a decorative divider), you should still include the `alt` attribute, but leave it empty: `alt=""`. This tells the screen reader to skip it entirely. ### Putting It All Together Here is a complete, real-world example of an `` tag you might use for a portfolio website: ```html ``` Notice that in this example, the `src` points to a folder named `images` inside the website directory, then to the file `my-artwork.jpg`. If you mix your HTML files, your image files, and your CSS files all in one single folder, your project will become messy and confusing. Best practice dictates that you create sub-folders to keep things organized. A typical website folder structure looks like this: * **website-folder/** * `index.html` * **images/** * `profile.jpg` * `artwork.png` * **css/** * `style.css` When your image is inside a folder, you must include the folder name in your `src` path, followed by a forward slash (`/`). Different formats are optimized for different types of visuals. When downloading or exporting images for your website, you will typically encounter three main formats: * **JPEG (or .jpg):** Best for photographs and images with millions of colors and complex shading. JPEGs compress the file size, which helps your web page load faster, but they do not support transparency. * **PNG:** Best for graphics, logos, and icons. PNGs support transparency (meaning you can see the background behind the image), but the file sizes are usually larger than JPEGs. * **GIF:** Best for simple animations. GIFs only support a limited color palette, so they aren't great for detailed photos. * **SVG:** Stands for Scalable Vector Graphics. Unlike the other formats, SVGs are made of mathematical code rather than pixels. They are perfect for logos and icons because they never lose quality, no matter how much you zoom in. For your personal website, you will likely use JPEGs for your profile photo and background pictures, and PNGs or SVGs for any logos or icons. ## Embedding External Media with ` ``` Unlike the `` tag, the `` tag. The Broken Image Icon** If you see a little torn picture icon instead of your image, your browser cannot find the file. * **The Fix:** Double-check your `src` path. Did you spell the file name exactly right? File names are case-sensitive, so `Profile.jpg` is different from `profile.jpg`. If your image is in a folder, ensure you included the folder name (e.g., `images/profile.jpg`). **2. Huge Images Breaking Your Layout** You took a photo on your phone and put it on your website, but it is so massive it takes up the entire screen and you have to scroll for miles just to get past it. * **The Fix:** While we will learn how to resize images perfectly using CSS in Chapter 4, you can prevent massive files from slowing down your site by resizing the actual image file before uploading it. Use a free tool or your computer's built-in photo editor to scale the image down to a reasonable width (like 1000 or 1500 pixels wide) before adding it to your `images` folder. **3. Missing `alt` or `title` Attributes** Your images load, but you forgot the accessibility text. * **The Fix:** Always go back and add `alt` to images and `title` to iframes.
